Details__c:
The purpose of this article is to guide you in understanding the basic information related to EPR (Extended Producer Responsibility) through the FAQ section, as well as to present the requirements for each country and how to meet your compliance obligations.

 

This does not constitute legal advice. You are encouraged to check your legal obligations and your compliance status with your legal advisor.  For more assistance, you can reach out to our partner Taxually.

 

I- Basics / Glossary:

  • EPR: Extended Producer Responsibility. This refers to a set of laws requiring sellers who market certain products to contribute to recycling and waste management under the polluter-pays principle.
  • WEEE or DEEE: Waste Electrical and Electronic Equipment or “Déchets d'Équipements Électriques et Électroniques” in French.
  • Eco fee: this is the fee amount due by the producer to the PRO for the products placed on the market. This is a statutory fee set by the PRO.  
  • Eco-ID or EPR number: This is the registration number of a producer with a PRO
  • PRO: Product Responsibility Organisation : it is the environmental agency in charge of a specific scheme (for example, WEEE, Packaging and so on) in a specific country.

II- FAQ:

What is Back Market's role regarding sellers ?

When facilitating the sale of products in certain countries, marketplaces may have a legal obligation to collect sellers' eco-IDs and verify them. If the seller does not provide their valid eco-IDs, , Back Market is required:

  • for products placed on the French market, to  pay eco-fees on behalf of sellers;
  • for products placed on the German market: to suspend your account from selling in Germany the categories of products for which you do not hold a valid eco-ID
  • for products placed on the Spanish market (Packaging): if your business is established outside of Spain and you do not provide a valid Spanish Packaging EPR registration number, Back Market may be legally required to report your packaging volumes to a PRO and to remit EPR fees on your behalf, then charge you accordingly (see “How does billing work for EPR?” below).

 

In which countries will Back Market require your EPR numbers ? 

France, Germany, Spain and Austria have imposed EPR obligations on marketplaces. Other countries may be added in the future.

For the moment, you can input your EPR numbers in your seller back office for France and for Germany. We will keep you informed as soon as we are in the capacity to collect data for other countries. Where the feature is available, you will be able to enter your EPR numbers directly in your Back Office.

 

In which categories will Back Market require your EPR numbers ?

You may require different EPR numbers depending on the categories of products that you place on the market. Back Market collects your EPR numbers for the product categories concerned in France and in Germany:

  • Packaging
  • Batteries
  • WEEE

You are only required to be registered for the categories of products that you place on the market. For example, if you do not place on the market any products containing batteries, you are not required to obtain an EPR number for the Batteries category.

 

If you place products on the Spanish market, you are required to obtain an EPR number for the Packaging category. Back Market will require your number and may verify it if you are not based in Spain. We will keep you informed when the back office is ready to collect this data (or when new requirements apply).

 

I have entered the EPR same number several times; is that acceptable/correct?

No, this is not correct. A different EPR number is required for each category (WEEE, Packaging, Batteries) and for each country. . This means you can upload up to six different EPR numbers :

 

  1. FR EPR Packaging number
  2. FR EPR Batteries number
  3. FR EPR WEEE number
  4. DE EPR Packaging number
  5. DE EPR Batteries number
  6. DE EPR WEEE number

If additional countries/categories are enabled in the Back Office (for example Spain Packaging), you will be required to provide a separate valid number for those as well.

 

What is the consequence if a  seller fails to provide their EPR numbers?

  • For France:
    Back Market will report your sales to the PROs on your behalf and will pay the eco-fees to the PROs. The seller will be charged by Back Market for these  fees for each order concerned and for each non-compliant category. See the question "How does billing work for EPR?" below.

 

  • For Germany:
    The Packaging category is mandatory for Germany. If the seller does not provide an EPR number for Packaging, they will be taken offline and will no longer be able to sell in Germany.
    You will also be required to share your EPR numbers for the WEEE and Batteries categories in order to be able to continue selling in Germany.

 

  • For Spain (Packaging):
    If your business is established outside of Spain and you do not provide a valid Spanish Packaging EPR registration number, Back Market is legally required to declare the packaging volumes you placed on the Spanish market to a PRO and to remit the corresponding EPR fees on your behalf. Back Market will then charge the EPR fees to your account, along with a reporting fee (where applicable).
    If your business is established in Spain, Back Market will not declare or pay on your behalf. You remain responsible for reporting your packaging volumes and paying EPR fees directly to your PRO and for complying with Spanish requirements.

How much are the EPR fees in France?

Please consult the official price lists of each PRO for detailed information (Article for EPR in France & Article for EPR in Germany). Depending on your specific situation and on the volume of products your place on the marketplace, you may be eligible to either the normal scheme or the simplified scheme.

 

As an example, the EPR fee applicable to smartphones sold from January 1st 2025 are :

 

  • For the WEEE category:
    • €4.42 for the simplified scheme
    • €2.56 for the normal scheme (€2.46 if the battery is removable by the user)
  • For the Packaging category: 0,1554 € for the simplified scheme. For the normal scheme : please refer to the PROs.
  • For the Batteries category: 0,30 € per battery for the simplified scheme. For the normal scheme : please refer to the PROs.

Where and how can sellers obtain their EPR numbers?

Sellers can obtain their EPR numbers from authorized PROs in their respective countries. In some situations, sellers may need to appoint a representative in order to register with a PROs.

For France:

 

  • Waste from Electrical and Electronic Equipment (WEEE)
    • https://www.ecosystem.eco
    • https://www.ecologic-france.com
  • Batteries
    • https://www.corepile.fr/
    • https://www.batribox.fr/
  • Packaging
    • https://www.citeo.com
    • https://www.leko-organisme.fr
    • Or reach out to our partner Taxually

 

For Germany:

 

  • Waste from Electrical and Electronic Equipment (WEEE)
    • https://www.stiftung-ear.de/en/home
  • Batteries
    • https://www.stiftung-ear.de/en/home
  • Packaging
    • https://lucid.verpackungsregister.org/
    • Or reach out to our partner Taxually

For Spain:

 

  • Packaging
    • https://www.ecoembes.com/en
    • https://procircular.es/
  • Sellers not established in Spain must also appoint an Authorized Representative to register and comply.
  • Or reach out to our partner Taxually

Where and how can sellers enter their EPR numbers?

Sellers can enter their EPR numbers in the Back Office under the "My Profile" section. You can then click on "Your business profile" and then "Legal details and documents." After that, scroll down to find the "Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR)" section, where you can submit your EPR numbers for both France and Germany, for the 3 required categories.

Where the Spain feature is available, you will be able to add your Spanish Packaging EPR registration number under the same EPR section by selecting Spain.

 

What is the format of an EPR number ?

  • In France: The correct format of FR EPR numbers should be the following:
    • FR EPR Packaging number: FRXXXXXX_XXXXXX
    • FR EPR Batteries number: FRXXXXXX_XXXXXX
    • FR EPR WEEE number: FRXXXXXX_XXXXXX

 

  • In Germany: The correct format of DE EPR numbers should be the following:
    • DE EPR Packaging number: DEXXXXXXXX (8 characters after “DE”)
    • DE EPR Batteries number: DEXXXXXXXXXXXXX (13 characters after “DE”)
    • DE EPR WEEE number: DEXXXXXXXX (8 characters after “DE”)

 

  • In Spain: The format of the Packaging EPR registration number may vary depending on the registration route/PRO and your setup (for example, via an Authorized Representative). Back Market may verify the validity of the number against official sources.

Back Market may verify the accuracy of the EPR numbers entered using the official databases.

 

Are second-hand products exempt?

Second hand products may be exempt provided they were already put on the local market and have therefore already been subject to EPR fees. For example, a smartphone that was already put in the market in France and was subject to France WEEE is exempt from WEEE when it is resold in France. Please note that imported products may not benefit from the exemption. For example, if you sell a second-hand smartphone in Germany that was sourced in France, the smartphone will still need to be subject to German WEEE. 

 

Exemptions do not apply to the Packaging category. Exemptions for second hand products can only apply to the WEEE and Batteries categories.

 

  • Sellers claiming exemption must indicate this in the dedicated "I am exempt" section in the Back Office under the WEEE/DEEE category.
  • If you already have communicated a WEEE/DEEE number in the format “FRXXXXXX_XXXXXXX”, no further action is needed.
  • We are currently working on implementing the same "I am exempt" feature in the Back Office, allowing you to inform us of an exemption for the Batteries category.

Does the seller need to provide specific documents at one stage of the process?

  • Exemptions: there is no document to upload, you only to tick the box "I am exempt" on the Back Office
  • For France: For France EPR numbers, there are no documents to upload.
  • For Germany: and for EPR Packaging only, we will ask you to upload a certificate of registration on top of the EPR number itself. This document should be uploaded in the Back Office directly.
  • For Spain: For Packaging, we require your Spanish Packaging EPR registration number (where applicable) and may verify it against official sources. No additional document upload is required unless communicated otherwise.

What resources can I use to get more information or check my compliance status?

  • SSC article: Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) : obligations in France
  • SSC article: Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR): Obligations in Germany
  • Check your Back Office directly (“My profile” > “Your business profile” > “Legal details and documents” > “"Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR)"), and check if you have communicated EPR numbers and if their format is correct.
  • We encourage you to contact our partner Taxually for assistance with EPR regulations, covering all the countries where Back Market operates.

How does billing work for EPR?

First of all, we encourage you to review our T&Cs, where all details related to EPR billing are outlined.

 

For France:

Back Market will report your sales and pay the EPR fees on your behalf, if you have not provided your valid EPR numbers for WEEE, Batteries and Packaging.  Back Market will charge these EPR fees reported on your behalf per category and on a quarterly basis. (e.g., billing for sales made in Q1, from January to March, will occur in April).

 

As a seller, if you become compliant in a specific category during the year, you are responsible for reporting the entire year directly to the PROs. Back Market does not handle this reporting; therefore, we provide retroactive refunds for EPR fees already billed for that year.

 

These retroactive refunds are also processed on a quarterly basis, occurring in the quarter following the submission of your EPR number and compliance in a given category. The line that will appear on your invoice will be as follows: “Regularization_EPR_France_Year_Quarter_WEEE/Packaging or Batteries”

 

In addition, Back Market will also charge a service fee called “EPR reporting fee” of € 50 per quarter and per category, for each category in which you have not provided your valid EPR number. These service fees are not refundable in case you become compliant during the year.

 

For Germany:

Back Market will not charge any eco-fees or any reporting fees to your account. Indeed, you are required to be compliant and to pay eco-fees yourself to the relevant PRO. If you are not compliant, Back Market is required to suspend your account in Germany.

 

For Spain:
 
If your business is established outside of Spain and you do not provide a valid Spanish Packaging EPR registration number, Back Market is legally required to declare packaging volumes placed on the Spanish market to a PRO, pay the corresponding EPR fees on your behalf, and charge these fees to your account (and a reporting fee where applicable). You will be charged based on the weight and composition of the packaging you put on the Spanish market.


 
If you provide a valid EPR number and it is validated, Back Market will stop declaring and invoicing packaging EPR fees for Spain. You remain responsible for declaring your packaging volumes and paying your EPR fees directly to your PRO.


 
If your business is established in Spain, Back Market will not declare or pay on your behalf. You remain responsible for reporting and payment directly to your PRO and for complying with Spanish requirements (including any applicable registry obligations).

 

III-Countries checklist requirements:

France EPR checklist:

  • Mandatory categories: WEEE, Packaging, and Batteries
  • Collection method: Sellers have to register, otherwise we report on your behalf and we take the money from your payout
  • Consequences if not compliant: you may incur fees (see the question "How much are the fees in France?")

Germany EPR checklist:

  • Mandatory categories: Packaging only for the moment
  • What to expect for the future: Enforcement of Batteries and WEEE most likely, as we did for Packaging
  • Collection method: No marketplace collection, but sellers are obliged to register themselves
  • Consequences if not compliant: If you do not provide us with an EPR number for the Packaging category, you will not be allowed to sell in Germany, and the market will be set to "offline" in your Back Office.

Spain EPR checklist:

  • Mandatory categories: Packaging (mandatory EPR obligations for packaging apply starting January 1, 2025)
  • What to expect for the future: Enforcement of Batteries and WEEE may follow
  • Collection method:
    • If your business is established in Spain: No marketplace collection; sellers are obliged to register and report themselves (including registering with the public producer registry, where applicable).
    • If your business is established outside of Spain: you must provide a valid Spanish Packaging EPR registration number; if you do not, Back Market may be legally required to declare/pay on your behalf and charge you accordingly.
    • Consequences if not compliant (outside Spain): Back Market will charge you the packaging EPR fees (and a reporting fee where applicable) based on packaging volumes placed on the Spanish market.
Title: EPR Countries requirements and most important questions

Details__c:
Before you start

Prefer a visual approach? You can create and manage your products directly through the Back Office, where you can easily fill in forms and see your changes in real time. This article is specifically for sellers who want to use API integration.

 

Whether you're using the API or the Back Office, always check if your product already exists in our catalog first. There's a good chance it's already there, which will save you time.

 

Creating Products via API

We highly recommend referring to our comprehensive API documentation for the latest information on creating products: Back Market - API Guidelines.

 

Getting Started

To make API calls with our database, you will need to enter an access token in your header. If you want to know how you can get your token, you can read the article Get your Token.

 

You can create a product with API on our marketplace by sending an HTTP POST request. The endpoint of the request is https://www.backmarket.fr/ws/listings.

 

Request Header

The header of your request should be (it's the same for all requests):

Content-type: application/json
Accept: application/json
Accept-Language: COUNTRY_CODE
Authorization: Basic YOUR_ACCESS_TOKEN

 

Request Body

In the Body of your request, you must put the equivalent of the CSV file used to create products.

 

There are product related fields and listing related fields. You must fill both types of fields because you create a product and a listing at the same time (you can't create a product without creating a listing on it).

 

Mandatory product related fields (the same for all categories):

 

  • the brand
  • the category ID
  • the description of the product
  • the image
  • the original price/price new
  • the title
  • the weight

Mandatory listing related fields (the same for all categories):

 

  • the EAN
  • the price
  • the quantity
  • the SKU
  • the state

For complete information on all fields required during product creation, please refer to our API documentation: Back Market - API Guidelines

 

Example Request Body

Finally, the Body of your request should have this form:

{
    "encoding": "latin1",
    "delimiter": ";",
    "quotechar": "\"",
    "header": true,
    "catalog": "ean;category_id;title;description;brand;price_new;weight;image1;color;sim_lock;screen_size;model;storage;sku;quantity;price;state;\n8018417227790;2;iPhone 7 White 64 Go;This is the iphone 7 of state super shiny description;Apple;960;325;http://images.neteven.com/evenmarketplace/images_telephone/iphone7.jpg;White;SFR;4,7;iPhone 7;64;iphone7sku1;25;700;0;\n8018417227790;2;iPhone 7 White 64 Go;This is the iphone 7 of state bronze description;Apple;960;325;http://images.neteven.com/evenmarketplace/images_telephone/iphone7.jpg;White;SFR;4,7;iPhone 7;64;iphone7sku2;20;600;3;\n0190198067456;1;Old school phone title;This is an old school phone of state super shiny description;Nokia;310;325;http://images.neteven.com/evenmarketplace/images_telephone/nokia.jpg;White;SFR;2,2;Nokia 32xx;;nokia32xxsku1;25;200;0;\n0190198067456;1;Old school phone title;This is an old school phone of state bronze  description;Nokia;310;325;http://images.neteven.com/evenmarketplace/images_telephone/nokia.jpg;White;SFR;2,2;Nokia 32xx;;nokia32xxsku2;10;140;3;\n"
}

 

Product Validation

Once your product is created, it will need to be validated by our team before your listing is set online. Validation typically takes between 24 and 72 hours, depending on current volumes and the number of requests from other sellers.
Title: Create my product with API

Details__c:
Back Market displays either 3 or 2 grades to our customers.

The three grades displayed to customers are:

  1. Fair
  2. Good
  3. Excellent

 

These grades take into account: the physical appearance of the device that you select when listing the product.
The 3 grades are based on the appearance of the body and screen of the device. 


Since July 2025, we also moved from 3 to 2 customer grades for screenless categories (headphones, accessories, household appliances), in order to provide more clarity to the customer. 

 

FYI: In Germany the following new wordings are shown to customers:

  • Fair = Gut
  • Good = Sehr Gut 
  • Excellent = Hervorragend

To know more about the physical appearance you can select when listing a product and the associated customer grades you can refer to our dedicated article in which you will also find Smartphone/Tablet and MacBook/Laptop Grading Toolkits.

 

How does it look to the customer?

To help educate the customer on each grade we have improved the customer experience by including not only written descriptions but also images. 

Our role as a marketplace is to adapt our model to the current market trends and be aligned with client expectations. This is why we moved our numbers of grades from five to three.
Title: Back Market's 3 Customer Grades

Details__c:
Disclaimer. This page has been added for general informational purposes only. It does not replace a comprehensive analysis of applicable product safety legislation, which is the responsibility of the sellers. The information provided is subject to change based on updates to the GPSR or related regulations. For questions about laws and regulations concerning your products and for targeted advice on how the General Product Safety Regulation (GPSR) and other regulations apply to your business, products, and listings, please consult your legal counsel. Back Market cannot provide legal advice.

 

Jump to: 

  • What does GPSR stand for and which territory and products are affected? 
  • What information related to product safety must be displayed ?
  • Who is the responsible person based in the EU ?
  • What does it mean for you?
  • What should the responsible person based in the EU do?
  • What product safety information must be labeled on the products?
  • What should sellers do in case of safety incidents?
  • When does the GPSR come into effect? Are there transitional implementation provisions for refurbished products ? 
  • What self-certification will be displayed by Back Market ?

 

 What does GPSR stand for and which territory and products are affected? 

The General Product Safety Regulation (EU) 2023/988 (“GPSR”) aims to ensure that products placed on the market in the EU meet high safety standards and comply with relevant legislation. GPSR has introduced new obligations related to the safety of the products offered online for sale to customers located in the EU and Northern Ireland. 

 

The GPSR is applicable to refurbished electronic products. However, it only applies to products placed on the EU market after December 13, 2024 (see below question n°7). 

 

What information related to product safety must be displayed ?

Marketplaces must allow sellers to display the following information for each product made available for sale on the platform:

 

  • The manufacturer’s contact details : brand (registered trademark), name, postal and electronic address at which the manufacturer can be contacted, and

  • If the manufacturer is not established in the EU or Northern Ireland, the responsible person based in the EU’s contact details: name (of the company or individual), postal and electronic address, (see question n°3 for more info )

  • Information allowing the identification of the product, including a picture of it, its type and any other product identifier,

  • Any warning or safety or information to be affixed on the product or to accompany it, in the language of the country of sale (or in a language easily understandable for customers).

The responsible person must be listed on the product packaging along with the manufacturer’s details.

 

Who is the responsible person based in the EU ?

The responsible person or entity, based in the EU or Northern Ireland, must fall under one of the following categories of economic operators:

 

  • If the manufacturer is based in the EU or Northern Ireland: the manufacturer itself serves as the responsible person.

  • If the manufacturer is outside the EU or Northern Ireland: either an importer or an authorized representative appointed by the manufacturer must assume this role.

  • If no manufacturer, importer, or authorized representative is established in the EU or Northern Ireland: a fulfillment service provider must act as the responsible person.

If the manufacturer of your products is outside the EU and/or if you are not the importer of the products within the EU, you can contact the manufacturer or your supplier to obtain the name and contact details of the responsible person based in the EU. If you still can’t identify it, third-parties providers (such as Webinterpret, Cert-Rep or Obelis) can help you identify a responsible person based in the EU. Note that these service providers are totally independent of Back Market which cannot be held liable for their services.

 

What does it mean for you?

 

 

If all of your manufacturers are in the EU, no additional information is needed. The manufacturer is the responsible person or operator, and the information will be provided by Back Market. 

Here are the steps to follow in that case: 

  • Step 1: Log into your Back Office > “Your business account” > “Legal details” tab.
  • Step 2: Select "Edit details".
  • Step 3: When a window appears, select the "All my products are manufactured within the EU or Northern Ireland" option.

 

 

If any of your manufacturers are outside the EU, add the contact information of your responsible person in your Back Office. 

Here are the steps to follow in that case:

  • Step 1: Log into your Back Office > “Your business account” > “Legal details” tab.
  • Step 2: Select "Edit details".
  • Step 3: When a window appears, select the "Some or all of my products are manufactured outside of the EU or Northern Ireland" option.
  • Step 4: Fill out the contact details of the responsible person or operator based in the EU.

The responsible person or operator will appear by default on all your listings, but you can change it for individual listings using a CSV file on the “Listings page”) (see below for more details).

If you want to update the contact details for the responsible operator for individual listings via CSV, here are the steps to follow in this case:

  • Step 1: Log into your Back Office >” Listings” > "Import or export listings".
  • Step 2: In the window that appears, choose the last option, ”Update contacts”.
  • Step 3: Create a blank CSV file and add these column headers:
    • sku
    • business_name
    • address_line_1
    • address_line_2
    • postal_code
    • city
    • country
    • phone
    • email
    • website
  • Step 4: Fill in the required details. For email and website fields, you’re only required to list 1 of the 2.
  • Step 5: Upload the completed CSV file in a UTF-8 format.

 

If you don't provide the information although some of your manufacturers are not based in the EU:

  • For EU-based sellers, your information will be provided by Back Market as the responsible person, meaning you will be considered the responsible operators and liable in case of safety issues.

  • For non-EU based sellers: Back Market may need to take your listings offline.

 

What should the responsible person based in the EU do?

The responsible person based in the EU is tasked with specific duties related to product compliance and safety, including:

 

  • Ensuring that the EU declaration of conformity, CE marking, and other technical documents are in order, and providing them to market surveillance authorities to prove product compliance.

  • Informing market surveillance authorities about dangerous products and accidents through the EU Safety Business Gateway.

  • Cooperating with market surveillance authorities and ensuring that necessary measures are taken to address any non-compliance issues.

  • Providing documented evidence of checks that have been carried out, if requested by the authorities.

 

What product safety information must be labeled on the products?

You must label your products with the following information:

 

  • The contact details of the responsible for the safety of the products:

    • manufacturer and,

    • if the manufacturer is not established in the EU or Northern Ireland, the contact details of the responsible person based in the EU,

  • Your contact details,

  • The type, batch, or serial number, and if applicable the product identificator number,

  • Safety information and warnings, where applicable, in the language of the country of sale, which includes the instructions of assembly and/or installation, use, and maintenance manuals.

 

If you do not have this information, you can contact the manufacturer.

You must ensure that the information is easily visible and legible for consumers.

This labeling can be provided on the product packaging or an accompanying document.

 

What should sellers do in case of safety incidents?

You must:

  • Take appropriate corrective actions if a product is found to be unsafe, such as warnings to customers and product recalls, in coordination with Back Market and relevant authorities, 

  • Ensure that necessary measures are taken to address any non-compliance issues. 

  • Facilitate any action taken to eliminate or, if that is not possible, to mitigate the risks presented by a product that is or was offered online. 

  • Cooperate with market surveillance authorities to communicate the relevant traceability information for the products to identify, as far as possible, the supply chain.

 

When does the GPSR come into effect? Are there transitional implementation provisions for refurbished products ? 

The GPSR only targets products placed on the EU market after December 13, 2024. If a product has already been imported (as new or refurbished) into the EU market before this date, it is not affected. Over time, all listings will eventually be affected.

 

What self-certification will be displayed by Back Market ?

As per our Sellers T&Cs, a self-certification, in which you acknowledge that you will sell on Back Market only products that comply with all applicable European safety regulations, will be displayed.
Title: GPSR: Main requirements related to products safety

Details__c:
... As a wizard does not reveal the recipe of his magic potion, we will not reveal all the aspects of the algo...🧙

However, as quality is strongly linked to your sales volume, we will of course give you everything you need to know so that you are able to maximize your profit.

Each product page on Back Market has three BackBoxes, one for each physical appearance: fair, good and excellent. Only one seller can sit on a buybox at a time, even when multiple sellers are pushing the same product in the same grade. Our customers will only see products with active BackBoxes. So, the key question is: How do you as a seller "win" the BackBox over competitors? 

 

On most other platforms, it’s exclusively about who offers the lowest price. On Back Market, price is taken into account, yes, but much more important is quality! Let us explain:

 

The BackBox system is based on a proprietary algorithm that calculates your price for a given listing using 10 different quality metrics that we track on a weekly basis:

 

  • You should be at ease with understanding all the different metrics of your quality dashboard by now.
  • The better you perform across these metrics relatively to the average on Back Market, the higher your "BackBox price" will be compared to other sellers competing for the same BackBox.
  • So, to sum up: Quality is fundamental to accessing the BackBoxes, and in effect, growing your business on Back Market.

 

The ultimate goal of our unique BackBox algorithm is to give the final customer a single offer that has the best quality/price ratio.
 


Good to know Q&A:

Which bank holidays are taken into account in the BackBox calculation?

Good question, mostly related to the Late Response and Delivery rate. Our algorithm takes of course into account the official bank holidays, working as the weekend (Saturday and Sunday), that will not count in the calculation. Please find enclosed the list of bank holidays our calculation is based on.
 

Can I export the price the win the BackBox?

Yes, you can export the price to win the BackBox for all your listings. This feature saves you time and helps you capture a larger share of BackBoxes. To do this, navigate to your Listings page, select Import or Export Listings, then choose Download Existing Listings via CSV. You’ll find the downloaded file in your File Library at the top right corner. Inside the CSV file, there is a column that lists all the prices needed to win the BackBox.

 

The customer cancels his order, is my cancellation rate impacted?

No, only orders cancelled by you without any reason and first client contact have a negative impact on your Quality score and therefore your BackBox access.
 

My quality is not good enough, I am not able to get any BackBox, but I don't know what to do.
No stress, liaise with your Back Market contact and explain the situation. Together you will work on an adapted action plan to help you improve. Feel free to ask about our services, which are really helpful to get a quick and efficient solution.
 

I have a big stock to sell, what’s the best strategy to adopt on Back Market?
We advise you to have a stable sales volume and grow it step by step. If you're making on average 100 sales a month, and you want to raise this volume up a notch, don’t rush it as it could lead to a poor after-sales management (and a strong impact on your access to BackBoxes). As the saying goes, it’s always better to be safe than sorry.
 

The competition & the quality score seems unfair to me.
All our sellers are subject to the same rules and calculations, meaning that our BackBox system stands on pillars of equity and fairness. Our algorithm calculation mainly focuses on client requests, which makes it strongly reality-oriented. What matters to us the most is to make our customers happy in order for them to come back.
 

My quality score is mostly impacted by wrong client requests. What can I do?
As already explained, all of our sellers may be impacted by false claims. This should however remain very rare and have a low impact on your quality score.  
That being said, if you need more details regarding those requests, please liaise with your Back Market contact, who will be able to give you a more detailed vision on your customers’ experiences.


How does the BackBox work in the Back Office ?
In the Listings page, you have the possibility to change your prices to reach the BackBox price.

 

In the picture below, you have 3 different prices :

 

  • BackBox Price: This is the price you must set to be visible on the platform.
  • Current Backbox Price: This is the price of the product currently visible on the platform.
  • My price: This is the price you have set for the product (or minimum and target price if you use BackPricer; for more information, see this article).

In order to win the BackBox, your price needs to be equal to the BackBox price.
Title: BackBox - How does it work?

Details__c:
The circularity rate is a Trade-in key performance indicator (KPI) that measures the percentage of products sold on Back Market that were sourced through Back Market Trade-in.

We measure this to ensure the Trade-in program is providing you with additional supply to boost your sales but also to ensure circularity within Back Market.

 

This KPI is one of the factors used in the Trade-in BackBox algorithm to help you maximize this virtuous circle.

 

Learn more about Trade-in BackBox.

 

How is it calculated?

This rate is recalculated every day to show how many Trade-in products you've bought and then sold on Back Market over the past 6 weeks. 
Basically, we count all the Trade-in devices you've paid for and then, we check how many of those devices you've sold on Back Market. As Back Market is not collecting unique identifiers for all products (e.g. IMEI for smartphones), the comparison is made based on the Back Market product ID. 

Note that:

 

  • This KPI includes a 1-week lag between the trade-in payment date and the selling date to reflect the average time to refurbish and sell back a device you sourced.
  • Non-functional devices that you source account only for 0.5 of a device. This means that sourcing 2 of such devices and reselling one would still get you a 100% circularity rate for these devices. The example below shows the calculation in action!

 

Example: Seller A sourced and paid for 100 iPhones 13, 128 GB between 1 April and 6 May. They sold 70 iPhone 13, 128 GB between 8 April and 13 May. Therefore, on 13 May, the Circularity Rate of Seller A for this product is 70%.

 

How to track your circularity rate?

You will be able to track your circularity rate from your Back Office shortly, by checking your Trade-in insights (Sales > Insights > Trade-in).

 

In the meantime, we invite you to track the products that you sell back on Back Market to improve your BackBox price.

 

How to improve your circularity rate?

Improving your trade-in circularity rate is fairly simple, you need to refurbish and resell the devices you sourced through Trade-in on Back Market.

 

We suggest you source devices with the best margin opportunities.
Title: Trade-in Circularity Rate

Details__c:
If you’d like to start selling Apple headphones, MacBook chargers, or Apple Pencils on Back Market, you’ll need to go through our approval process. Note that rules have been updated in August 2023 for Apple headphones in particular. 

While all our sellers on Back Market have proven their high standards, many original suppliers attempt to sell counterfeit devices in these categories. This means you’ll need to make sure your source is 100% reliable and educate yourself on the risks of counterfeit items before you apply to sell them on Back Market.

Check out this article to learn more about testing for counterfeit AirPods

In order to apply, follow these steps.


Jump to

Apple headphones (AirPod, AirPod Pro, AirPod Max)
MacBook chargers and Apple Pencils


Apple headphones (AirPod, AirPod Pro, AirPod Max)

Please note that these guidelines were updated in August 2023 due to high levels of potential counterfeit and defective devices. 

To get approved to sell Apple headphones (including AirPod, AirPod Pro, and AirPod Max), you must have:

  • Overall sales of 650.000€ (650K) or above in the 90 days prior to your application date.
  • A history of selling on the platform for more than 6 months.

When you meet these qualifications, submit an application form. Here's how:

 

  1. Complete the application form on Google Sheets
  2. Review the 10-control-point test linked in the Google Sheet. Make sure you’re using it when testing your AirPods.
  3. Send a request through the Seller Support Center.
  4. When asked if your request is order related, select “No”.
  5. When asked what your request is about, click “Listings”.
  6. When asked what you want to do, click “Apply to sell restricted items”.
  7. When asked for the category, click “AirPods”.
  8. Attach your completed application form to the request.


MacBook chargers and Apple Pencils

  1. Send a sample to Back Market’s Innovation Labs in either New York or Bordeaux. Make sure to keep the tracking number and proof of purchase.

Back Market Innovation Lab

New York, NY 10003

 

Back Market Innovation Lab

123 Rue Lucien Faure

33300 Bordeaux

2. Submit a request through the Seller Support Center.

3. When asked if your request is order related, select “No”.

4. When asked what your request is about, select “Listings”.

5. When asked what you want to do, select “Apply to sell restricted items”.

6. When asked for the category you want to sell, select “MacBook chargers or Apple Pencils.”

7. Include your tracking number and receipt or proof of purchase in the request.

Details__c:
At Back Market, we’re always looking for ways to raise the bar for transparency and accuracy ; especially in how we measure seller quality.

 

That’s why we introduced AI help request classification, a system that uses artificial intelligence to analyze customer claims and categorize them more accurately. The result is fairer quality metrics, better insights into what’s actually happening, and fewer cases where performance is impacted by customer misclassification.

 

 

What is help request classification ?

When a customer contacts Support and opens an after-sales claim, we use AI to classify the help request by analyzing the conversation thread, the messages exchanged between the customer and our agents.

 

This classification feeds into quality KPIs such as :

 

  • Defective rate

  • Wrong product rate

  • Aesthetic rate

  • And other claim-related indicators

Historically, our system relied heavily on the issue type selected by the customer at claim creation.

 

Those are useful, but in practice they often don’t reflect the full reality of the situation.

 

 

Why we made this change

Real conversations are rarely simple. We observed several recurring issues that made our quality measurement less precise than we wanted.

 

Customers sometimes :

 

  • selected an issue type that didn’t match the real problem ; for example, a technical issue filed as “appearance”
  • opened claims based on misunderstandings of policies or what’s included
  • mentioned multiple issues in the same claim, but only one category was recorded
  • started with a delivery issue, then revealed a technical and/or appearance issue later in the thread

All of this meant our seller quality metrics weren’t always as complete, or as fair, as they should be.

 

 

What this means in practice

Instead of relying only on the initial tile, the AI analyzes the full claim conversation to capture the right issue(s) ; including cases where the problem evolves over time.

 

In practice, this brings :

 

  • More accurate metrics: based on what actually happened

  • Fairer outcomes for sellers: less impacted by customer mistakes or misunderstandings

  • Deeper insights: especially when multiple issues appear in a single thread

Please note: in the Customer Care section and customer conversations, you will still see the issue selected by the customer when opening the claim. The AI classification is what’s used for quality measurement and KPI calculation.

 

 

Rollout and updates

The classification system launched on 20 October 2025 and reviewed claims dating back to January 2025. Claims were re-categorized and tagged with the appropriate issue when necessary, and this re-categorization can shift seller quality KPIs.


Because this shift could impact quality-related suspensions, we implemented a 30-day adjustment period after rollout with no new suspensions ; to give sellers time to review changes and identify patterns.

 

Thanks to feedback and testing from sellers and Seller Success teams, we improved the model to make categorization even more reliable.

 

 

Accuracy improvements

AI classification currently reaches 80% accuracy, compared to 58% for customer self-classification. Recent model improvements increased overall AI accuracy from 72% to 80%.

 

The biggest gain is on Wrong Product :

 

  • accuracy improved from 46% to 77%

These improvements were applied retroactively to all conversations starting January 1, 2025 ; meaning Back Office KPIs are already up to date.

 

 

Main classification changes in the updated model

1. Bad Product is no longer used when the issue isn’t the seller’s responsibility

 

These cases are now classified as Other. For example :

 

  • a customer says a phone arrived “without a case,” but the case is a separate order line shipped in another parcel
  • missing iPad chargers are no longer “Bad Product,” since chargers are no longer expected from sellers

 

2. Defective is no longer used when the issue isn’t the seller’s responsibility

 

These cases are now classified as Other. For example :

 

  • a customer orders an eSIM and complains about it
  • a customer complains about battery health even though it’s above 85% (or 80% in the UK and US)

 

3. Clearer distinction between similar issues

 

We improved how the model separates :

 

  • delivery issues
  • return / BackShip issues
  • change of mind / customer preference

 

4. Better handling of multiple issues in one thread

 

If a help request includes two issues ; for example a delivery problem followed by a defective issue ; the model now detects this more reliably via multiple contact counting.

 

 

What to do if you encounter an incorrect classification

If you believe a claim has been misclassified, please contact the Seller’s Support Center.

 

 

What’s next

We’ll keep improving AI help request classification throughout the year and share updates when changes go live. If you still notice misclassifications, please continue sharing them ; your feedback directly helps improve the system.
Title: AI help request classification: fairer, more accurate quality measurement with AI

Details__c:
Online fraud is a real problem these days with all the new tech, ways to pay, and how data are handled. It's something we have to watch out for. At Back Market, we take it seriously and have dedicated teams to support you. This guide explains the different types of fraud you may encounter, how Back Market supports you, and what you can do to help us assist you effectively.

 

Types of fraud

There are different kinds of fraud, and knowing which one you're dealing with is key. Online fraud generally falls into three main categories:

 

Payment fraud

This happens when someone uses stolen or compromised card details to place an order, without the cardholder's knowledge or consent. The legitimate cardholder will typically dispute the transaction with their bank.

 

Signals to watch out for:

 

  • Multiple orders placed in a short period of time using different accounts but the same delivery address.
  • Multiple orders placed in a short period of time by the same account, not consistent with legitimate buyer behaviour.
  • A customer asking to change the delivery address after the order is placed. 

Abuse of commercial chargebacks

A chargeback is a dispute raised directly with a bank or payment provider, where the customer asks for a transaction to be reversed. It is a legitimate consumer protection tool, but it can be misused. Abuse happens when a customer deliberately opens a claim on a transaction that has been properly fulfilled, for example by falsely claiming they never received their order, that it didn't match the description, or that they returned the item for a refund.

 

Signals to watch out for:

 

  • A pattern of disputes from the same customer across multiple orders.
  • A customer who raises a chargeback very shortly after receiving the order, with no prior contact with customer service.

Refund abuse 

This occurs when a customer exploits the return or refund process without a valid reason, for example by claiming a product is defective or missing when it isn't. This could also involve manipulation of the delivery process, such as false non-delivery claims, address tampering on return shipping labels, or interception of parcels.

 

Signals to watch out for:

 

  • A return is received but the product inside doesn't match what was originally shipped (different item, wrong IMEI).
  • The return package arrives empty or with an item of no value inside.
  • A return parcel whose tracking shows it traveling to unexpected locations or being scanned multiple times in unusual sequence.

Keep in mind: not every complaint is fraud. A customer raising an issue is not the same as someone trying to scam you. We need solid evidence to treat a case or customer as fraudulent.

 

How to stay protected

Back Market has your back

As long as you are acting in line with Back Market's Seller’s Terms & Conditions and Quality Charter, we will shield you from any losses from fraud. Your cooperation during our investigation remains essential though. The more complete your evidence and timely your response, the better our chances of preventing fraud together.

 

Best practices

Following these best practices helps reduce your exposure to fraud and ensures we can support you effectively:

 

  • Ship orders on time as promised.
  • Always send the order to the address provided at the time of purchase, even if the customer later asks you to change it.
  • Never communicate with a customer outside of Back Market.
  • Share delivery tracking number with Back Market so the shipment can be monitored.
  • Keep IMEI or serial number records confirming which specific device was shipped.
  • Use a shipping provider that requires a signature upon delivery where possible.
  • Store and provide shipment information like proof of delivery (including a signature if applicable), parcel weight records confirming the package matched the expected product weight, alerts on suspicious activity from shipping providers, etc.
  • Reply to any requests from Back Market within 24 working hours.

Handling suspicious activity

If something feels off about an order, don't ignore it. Report it to Back Market straight away via the support request form: After-sales management > Report suspected customer fraud

 

If our team contacts you about a suspicious transaction, follow the steps below depending on the order status:

 

  • Order not yet shipped? Hold off on shipping and cancel the order.
  • Order already shipped? Contact your shipping provider as soon as possible to attempt to stop or redirect the delivery.
  • Order returned to you? If the product came back because the customer's address didn't exist or was invalid, confirm receipt and reply directly to the Back Market agent
Title: How to spot and protect yourself from fraud

Details__c:
What is a Credit Request ?


A Credit Request is a way of sending money to the customer's virtual wallet via a Back Market Tool in the Back Office. From this virtual wallet, the customer can at any time transfer all or part of their virtual wallet to their personal bank account, free of charge.
 

Which cases can a Credit Request be made?


The Quality Charter details all the cases when Back Market Agent will proceed with a goodwill gesture. A goodwill gesture means any gesture to compensate the customers. It can refer to a partial refund or a credit request, or other advantages offered to the customer. 


For sales 

Back Market Agents follow strict guidelines on Credit Requests outlined in the section 3.6 Goodwill gestures. Credit Requests prevail over partial refund for: missing, incompatible, defective accessories (see paragraph 3.6.2), shipping delays (see paragraph 3.6.1), in case of a refund failed for an order paid with Paypal (see paragraph 3.5.5), missing IMEI/Serial Number (see paragraph 2.2) or missing invoice (see paragraph 2.3), cover solutions offered within the seller warranty (see paragraph 3.4.2). 

 

To increase the probability that the customer will keep the device, for all partial refunds or goodwill gestures, the amount will be rounded up to the nearest 5$. For example, a refund of 7$ will be rounded up to 10$. This does not apply to partial refunds for late express shipping, which will be refunded at the exact shipping cost.

 

The minimum commercial gesture is 5 €/$/£.

 
Below are information from the Quality Charter: 

3.6.1 IN CASE OF LATE SHIPPING
If your customer’s delivery is delayed by 1 or more days, whether due to delays on your part or a shipping provider’s, your customer is entitled to a refund equivalent to the shipping cost. 

3.5.5 If a refund failed for an order paid with PayPal
Back Market will make a goodwill gesture at the seller’s expense in order to refund the customer and inform both the seller and the customer in the customer request discussion.

This is most of the time due to insufficient amounts on the PayPal account. In that case, a credit request is made at the seller’s expense and the refund should not be made via PayPal.

2.3 What happens if you don’t provide the IMEI or serial number ? 
If a customer requests the IMEI or serial number at any time, you will have1 business day to provide it. If you haven’t provided the number after 24 hours, Back Market may offer your customer a goodwill gesture valued up to 50€/$/£ or an order cancellation.

2.4 What happens if you don’t send an invoice ?
If you don’t send the invoice within 2 business days following Back Market’s reminder, the customer is entitled to a free cancellation of their order or a goodwill gesture valued at up to €50/$50/£50.

3.4.2 SELLER WARRANTY : SOLUTIONS TO OFFER
You can offer return assistance and/or alternative solutions if appropriate or if your customer clearly asks for other options. This can include simple remote troubleshooting, offering a goodwill gesture in accordance with the Quality Charter requirements, or sending a new removable battery when it can be easily changed by your customer.
 

This means: covering some repair costs with a goodwill gesture.

 

3.6.2 In case of missing or defective accessories 
In the event of a missing or defective standard accessory, the agent will offer your customer a choice between the following options:

 

  • A goodwill gesture according to the amounts indicated in table below
  • The missing accessory

For Trade-in


2.5.3 MAKING A COUNTEROFFER
Counteroffers must be handled with care and tact, and you must provide as much information to the customer as possible so they fully understand why the amount is different from the original amount.
Back Market may pay the customer the initial price.

Back Market may use Credit Request tool charged at your expense in the following situations:
 

Unjustified counter-offer

(displayed as “Refurbisher charter breach”)

You made a counter-offer that is clearly in violation of the rules listed in the Charter.
The customer shall be compensated so that they are paid the initial offer price.

Device false statement (displayed as “Refurbisher charter breach”)

You made a false statement about the device (e.g. wrong model not declared, wrong IMEI provided), which is at breach with quality charter. The customer shall be compensated so that they are paid the actual value of their device. 
Return feesYou charged the customer for the shipping fees after a counter-offer refusal. The fees charged to the customer will be refunded. 
Missing accessories when device is sent back to customerYou did not send the accessories back to the customer. The value of commercial gesture is determined thanks to the grid available in 3.6.2 section of Sales part of the Quality charter.


Where can I follow the Credit Requests I will be charged for? 


You can follow the Goodwill gestures at your expense in your Back Office.

 

Where can I dispute commercial gestures?

If you wish to dispute a credit request, please provide all the necessary information (at least the commercial gesture number) by submitting a request to the Seller Support Center.

Please ensure that your request is legitimate before sending it to the Seller Support Center. Otherwise, your request will not be processed by our teams.
Title: Credit Requests charged to Sellers

Details__c:
Congratulations, your account is now activated, and you are ready to sell on Back Market! You are entering the Launch Phase.

In this article, we’ll summarize the information you need to know about this phase.
 


What’s the Launch Phase? 

The Launch Phase is a crucial step for sellers on Back Market.

It consists of giving you all the knowledge and tools you need to get off to a good start including: setting up your seller account, meeting our quality requirements, managing your finances, and improving your sales on the platform. 

Over a period of 30 days, you’ll be limited to a set number of sales per day, and you’ll have to complete four different training courses, in a specific order, with a test at the end. Once each training course has been completed, your daily sales limit will increase.

The four training courses mentioned above are:
 
  • Back Office setup Office 
  • Quality and Seller Support Center
  • Finance and Legal
  • Key success factors

Keep in mind, your launch phase is time-bound. This means you need to be prepared with items to sell on day one.

To help you visualise the progress of your launch phase, here are the concrete details of the different stages through which you will progress:
 

Day 1 to 30:
 
  • Complete “The configuration of your Back Office" to increase your sales limitation from 1 to 3 sales per day.
  • Complete "Quality and seller support center" to unlock 4 sales per day.
  • Complete "Finance and legal" to unlock 6 sales per day.
  • Complete "Key success factors" to unlock 10 sales per day. 

On Day 15:

If you have completed all 4 training courses, your quality and delivery performance will be evaluated and either:
 
  • Rewarded: increase to 15 sales per day.
  • Rejected: sales limitation unchanged

On Day 30:

After 30 days, there will be a final evaluation of your performance, where you can succeed or fail the Launch Phase.
 
⚠️ Please note that if the four different trainings have not been completed and passed by this day, your account will be closed. 

If your account succeeds, the daily sales limit will be further increased and you will gain access to all Back Market services. 
 

How to validate your Launch Phase ?

The first condition to validate your launch phase is to complete and pass all the training courses within the 30 days allowed. 

The second condition is to maintain a high sales volume while achieving strong results across all performance indicators. These must meet our quality thresholds described below: 
  • On-Time Delivery Rate (OTDR)
To pass your Launch Phase, your On-Time Delivery Rate must be 90% or more.
 
  • Defective Rate
To pass your Launch Phase, your Defective Rate must be 5% or lower.
 
  • Appearance Rate
To pass your Launch Phase, your Appearance Rate must be 1.5% or lower.
 
  • Order Cancellation Rate
To pass your Launch Phase, your Order Cancellation Rate must be 2% or lower.
 
  • Wrong Product Rate
To pass your Launch Phase, your Wrong Product Rate must be 2% or lower.
 
  • Volume of sales 
To pass your Launch Phase, you need to deliver a significant number of sales so we can collect enough data to assess your quality metrics during our quality checks.

 

To start with the first training, click here Back Office setup Office.
Title: #1 - Launch Phase Presentation

Details__c:
Since June 2021, sellers have been in charge of generating return labels for customers in case they refuse your counteroffer. Going forward, you’ll be able to ask for a refund of those return costs directly in the Seller Support Center. The exact conditions to do so are mentioned in this article.
 

How to get a refund of return costs

This process is only valid for refused counteroffers (and can NOT be applied to authentication/non-European issues).

When an order is canceled after a counteroffer, you have to generate a shipping label, send the item back to the customer within 2 business days, and provide them with the tracking number in the after-sales conversation.

You can send the list of orders where you had to generate a shipping label by creating a ticket in the Seller Support Center by following these exact steps:

  1. Go to the Seller Support Center and submit a new request
  2. Select "Is your question order related?" > "Yes" > "Trade-in order"
  3. In the list, select "Request a refund of return costs"
  4. Click "Next"
  5. Provide all order numbers involved in this refund request


The results will be sent directly to the Trade-in Team who will proceed with your request.

Our team from the Seller Support Center will check your requests and confirm that all conditions were respected with the finance team.
 

How much will Back Market refund?

The amount that will be refunded depends on the product category and the country. Take a look at the price grid that was defined by our Trade-in team. 



 

How does Back Market determine these prices?

Example for a smartphone (from seller in France to customer in France):

  • The price is fixed based on the average shipping provider’s price for a 250g package without insurance.
  • You can use the original package provided by the customer. Please make sure to return the package in the correct condition to avoid any problems during transport.

 

How to get the return costs refunded?

  • Send a request to the Seller Support Center within 3 months of the reception date.
  • Send the message within 2 business days of the cancellation date.
  • Provide a photo of the device in the after-sales platform before shipment.
  • Send the tracking number in the after-sales management page (using the word "tracking", “suivi”, “seguimiento”, “rastreo”, "Sendungsverfolgung", "Sendungsverfolg" or "Sendungsnummer" in the template). You can use the templates attached to this article. 

 

When will I receive the refund?

All requests provided within each month will be refunded on the 6th of the next month.

For example, if you send us a request on October 22, we will refund you on the invoicing period of November 6. If you ask for a refund on November 5, we will refund you the month after, on  December 6.

The refunds will be invoiced as "buyback_logistics_refund".

 

What happens if the item is lost during shipping?

If the item sent back is lost or broken during shipping, Back Market will reimburse the customer via a credit request at the original price 72 hours after the issue has been reported by the customer (if proof has been given).
Title: Return process in case of Trade-in order cancellations

Details__c:
Counteroffers are very critical to customer satisfaction and require extra effort from you, which is why you need to avoid them whenever possible. It’s recommended that you counteroffer in only 20% to 25% of received orders.

 

 

How is my Counteroffer Rate calculated?

The Counteroffer Rate measures the percentage of orders that you received and for which you made a counteroffer (CO) to the customer.

This is how the Counteroffer rate is computed: We count all the CO you made for the orders received in the last 30 days. We add an estimation of still-undeclared CO for your orders pending processing or pending a customer action by applying your raw CO rate* to them. We divide by the number of orders you received in the last 30 days.

The Counteroffer rate is calculated every day.

 

*raw CO rate is the old definition of the CO rate, computed as: Number of CO you made for the orders received in the last 30 days, divided by the number of orders you received in the last 30 days.

 

 

When NOT to make a counteroffer?

Counteroffers must be justified, fair in value, and can only be made rarely. Therefore, there are various cases in which counteroffer is not permitted.

 

Non-legitimate CO reason

Based on the customer declaration for their device, and the grade associated, you have reasons of CO not allowed for each device grade (e.g. If the customer has declared their phone broken, you cannot make a CO reporting it is broken).

 

Access here the full list of allowed – and not allowed – CO reasons for each grade and each product category:


Click here to access the list in English.

Click here to access the list in German.

Click here to access the list in Spanish.

 

Learn more about grading guidelines.

 

Notably, you cannot make a counteroffer for:

 

  • Grade 0 items (Functional - Flawless) with few faint signs of wear
  • Grade 1 items (Functional - Very good) with deep scratches
  • Grade 5 items (Not functional — broken parts with cracks)
  • Grade 4 items (Not functional — broken parts with scratches) except in cases of broken screens, broken back panels, and / or broken side panels
  • Unofficial parts as items sold by Back Market customers have had previous lives and may have been refurbished
  • Missing accessories if they're not mandatory
  • Accessories that are not broken

For EU refurbishers, a non-European item isn’t a sufficient reason to refuse it or to make a counteroffer

 

Non-spottable issues

Do not make a counteroffer for non-spottable issues. A non-spottable issue is a technical issue that can't be spotted by a person without special skills in device repair. Non-spottable issues can be present on all features of an item.

Here are just a few examples of non-spottable issues:

 

  • False contact at the charging connector
  • Weak vibration intensity
  • Muffled microphone audio
  • Small black spot on the rear camera

How to make a counteroffer?

When you have no other possibility, here’s our guideline to make a counteroffer:

 

Set a reasonable price for your counteroffer

Make sure the counteroffer price is consistent with:

 

  • Initial price
  • Standard industry costs to repair the damage
  • Your price for the new grade of the item, or Back Market prices for new grade of the item


ℹ️ Remember that likeliness of the customer to accept the counteroffer is heavily dependent on the amount of your counteroffer

 

Justify it clearly and accurately

Counteroffers must be handled with care and tact. You must send the Back Market customer as much information as possible, so they fully understand why the counter-offered amount is different from the original amount.

When making a counteroffer, you must:

 

  • Select the right reason(s) that justify the counteroffer among the list provided

⚠️ All reasons are displayed from your Back Office. Be careful on choosing  one/several reasons which are allowed for the grade of the device you received

 

  • Add at least one piece of evidence (photo, video)
    • If the counteroffer is linked to the appearance of the item, include high-quality photos of all damage that justifies a lower appearance grade
    • If the counteroffer is linked to the functional condition of the item, include evidence of each functional problem such as a testing software report or video
  • Add comments so that customers understand why they are counteroffered at a lower price. 

Here are our top recommendations for counteroffer evidence:

Set up a place where you can take clear, high-quality photos:

 

  • Take pictures against a white background
  • Use consistent, uniform lighting on all of your pictures (see this tool for suggested lighting) 
  • Use a pointer to point out any defects to make your picture as self-explanatory as possible

Save time when communicating pieces of evidence between your quality and care teams:

 

  • Update all proof to the cloud so that your care team can easily retrieve evidence when chatting with Back Market customers (use a cloud-based solution like Google Drive or OneDrive)
  • Organize all evidence by order number so that customer care teams can easily find the proof

Choose well-known software for quality testing:

 

  • We recommend Blancco, PhoneCheck, and NSYS Group 
  • If you use other software, let us know, and we’ll update our recommendations

ℹ️ Remember that likeliness of the customer to accept the counteroffer is impacted by the clarity of the counteroffer.

 

Note that Back Market may pay the Back Market customer the initial price if you don't submit sufficient evidence to justify a counteroffer or make an non-legitimate counter offer.

 

 

What happens if my Counteroffer rate is too high?

The higher your counteroffer rate is, the higher the prices for sourcing will be for you. Indeed, since the launch of the Trade-in BackBox as of June 2025, you are rewarded for having a lower counteroffer rate than your competitors by being able to access the BackBox for a lower price. Learn more about the Trade-in BackBox.


Also, if your Counteroffer Rate is too high, you’ll enter a “monitoring period" where you have the opportunity to improve your rate. If your rate doesn’t improve enough, your daily Trade-in limit will be decreased. If the issue persists, you won’t be able to use the Trade-in service.

​​​​​​​Learn more about Counteroffer Rate thresholds and how the monitoring period works.

 

 

How do I keep my Counteroffer Rate low?

Check your Back Office frequently to monitor your counteroffer rate


Optimize your offer

 

  • Set your listing price with the updated cosmetic and functional definitions in mind. (See 2. and 3.)
  • The older the model, the lower the chance you have of receiving devices without scratches.
  • Diversify your inventory by buying multiple cosmetic and functional grades.
  • Optimize your Trade-in offer prices by analyzing the profitability of your recent Trade-in orders.


Apply our operational recommendations

  • Read the grading guidelines carefully, print and share with your teams. (Print pages with 🖨️ in the lower right corner). Learn more and access grading guidelines.
  • Don't be too "picky" about cosmetic or low-repair-cost functional issues.
  • Remember, there is no link between Trade-in and Sales grading.


Remember that Trade-in grading isn't the same as the sales appearance grades.
 

 

How should I monitor my performance indicators?

You can check the progress of your Counteroffer Rate and other performance indicators in your Trade-in insights dashboard. You can access these from your Sales Back Office, then “Insights” > “Trade-in”.


If you have any questions, contact your Back Market point of contact or reach out via the Seller Support Center.
Title: What’s my counteroffer rate, and how do I keep it low?

Details__c:
Setting up your Back Office is one of the most important steps to start selling on Back Market.

Each of the following points is essential for a successful start on the platform and will help you answer the test at the end of this training to increase your current daily sales limit from 1 to 3.
 

 

Products and listings

To understand how our marketplace works, first learn the differences between a product and a listing.

 

  • A product refers to a device with several attributes (model, brand, color, weight, operator, processor, etc.). A product can be used by several sellers on Back Market. It only needs to be created once in the catalog.
  • A listing refers to the offer that a seller makes on this product. It is up to you to create your listing for an existing product.


Learn more about the difference between products and listings

 

Create your listings

In order for your account to be live, create your listings with up-to-date prices and stock amounts. You can add your listings manually, through a CSV file or even connect your API to do it automatically.
Learn how to create listings

 

Create your products

If your product doesn’t already exist in our catalog, or if no similar product exists to duplicate, you need to create the product.
Learn how to create products in the Back Office

 

Avoid mistakes in creating your listings and products

Properly creating or updating your listings and products on our marketplace is key. 

If you encounter any errors during these processes, contact the Seller Support Center immediately as errors can mean a loss of potential sales for you and your business.

 

 

Note that once you have created a product, it needs to be validated by our catalogue team which can take up to 48 hours. 

When choosing your listings’ grades, make sure your grading system is matching Back Market’s standards.

Learn about the physical appearance grades.

 

Shipping methods

In order for your listings to go live once created, you need to add shipping methods for each market. If you don’t add any shipping method, your ads will remain offline. 
Learn more about setting up shipping methods

Your shipping methods need to meet our delivery requirements, especially in terms of delivery times for each category.
Learn more about delivery requirements
 


The BackBox

To sell on Back Market, your listed products need to be visible to customers. To do this, you need to win what we call The BackBox, our in-house algorithm which advertises to customers the best quality-price ratio for each listing on the website. To understand more about the BackBox, please watch the video below. 
Learn the ins and outs of The BackBox
 

 

Our Seller Support Center

Our Seller Support Center (SSC) is your best ally at Back Market. It’s here to answer all your questions and provide you with daily support.
Learn how the Seller Support Center works

The Seller Support Center serves as your main point of contact. Use it as a go-to for all questions relating to your activity, sales, performance, any particular order, or any other Back Market services.
Learn how to contact us

 

 

Manage your Customer Care tab

By clicking on the "Customer Care" tab in your Back Office, you’ll arrive in the after-sales area, which is composed of four main tabs:

 

  • Open tasks
  • All requests

Keep in mind that Back Market will manage all your customer requests and will only ask you to take action when necessary through a notification in the Back Office. 

Learn more about Customer Care by Back Market
 

 

Manage your orders

When it comes to managing your orders, we have plenty of resources. Start with the order page, this is where you’ll find order support and process your orders.
Learn how the orders page works

Please note that you must provide the IMEI of every smartphone sold on Back Market in the Back Office within one working day after shipment. The IMEI must also appear on the invoice.
Learn how to fill in IMEIs

Please note our other guides for managing your orders:

 

  • Manage transport issues
  • Manage return requests
  • Manage full or partial refunds

 


How do I complete this module?

To complete this module, please answer this 3-minute form. If you pass, your sales limitation will automatically increase from 1 to 3 sales per day.

To continue with the next training, click here Quality and Seller Support Center.
Title: #2 - Back Office setup

Details__c:
In the event that you need to refund a customer for an order they placed on Back Market, reference the following article to make sure you are refunding correctly. 
 

Where can I refund an order ?

You can refund an order in the corresponding after-sales request by simply clicking on 'Refund'. This will open a pop-up, where you will input the amount you want to refund to the client. Validate the refund after having indicated the correct amount. Please note that shipping fees must be included in a full refund and cannot be removed from the amount.

 
 

Full or Partial Refund?

In the event of a full refund, the order must be totally reimbursed, including: 

 

  • The amount of the order including all taxes
  • The shipping costs paid by the customer at the time of the order
  • Any other costs necessary for your customer to return the product

You can’t deduct the following costs from the refund:

 

  • Restocking fees
  • Return costs in case your customer refuses the package
  • Missing accessories - request compensation from the SSC in these cases

Two allowed reasons exist for withholding part of a full refund:

 

  • Degraded products returned for a change of mind (withdrawal)
  • Confirmed warranty exclusions

When issuing a partial refund, you will be able to choose the amount you want to refund.
 

  • For example, if the device costs €260, and you are allowed to retain €5, simply indicate €255 instead of €260

This same rule applies also if you need to refund only €10 for example. In that case, please indicate €10 in the amount to refund. Our system will automatically take into account that €10 was already refunded. If a few days or weeks later the full order needs to be refunded, the already refunded amount (€10), will be taken into account and not be billed twice.


In any case, clicking on a 'Refund' button will always open a validation page to check the amount and order. Unfortunately, once the refund has been validated it cannot be reversed. 
 

What happens if a refund failed for an order paid with PayPal?

Back Market will make a goodwill gesture at the seller's expense in order to refund the customer and inform both the seller and the customer in the service request discussion.
 

Where to view all refunded orders? 

You will view all refunded orders directly on the corresponding invoice. For example, on your invoice of November 26, you will see all orders refunded before November 26, starting on November 16 (last billing date).

You can also view all refunded orders in the Customer Care Insights Dashboard, available in your Back Office.
Title: How do I issue a full or partial refund?

Details__c:
Charger compliance with International Safety Standard IEC 62368-1:

As a seller, you are accountable for the safety of the accessories you provide on the platform as stated by the law and reminded in our T&Cs [articles 8, 12, 13, and 14]. 

Therefore, you are entitled to document your charger compliance with the International Safety Standard IEC 62368-1 as detailed below.

Note that this documentation can be asked by BackMarket at all times, especially in case of repeated occurrences of potentially hazardous chargers being delivered to customers (e.g. charger that overheated or burnt).

 

You will receive a warning at the first case identified; as it could be a one-off no action would be taken until a second case is reported.

In the event that you have several identified cases, you will be required to provide the documentation listed here below within 30 days for all chargers you provide on our Platform. If you fail to prove their conformity within due delays, Back Market could take all appropriate measures, including sales restriction, suspension and closing of your account.

 

List of documents required to prove compliance with the International Safety Standard IEC 62368-1:

  • The full test report LVD for each charger model: The test report LVD should be in accordance with the standard IEC 62368-1 (the new mandatory standard since December 2020) - see Appendix 1
  • The following 4–5 pictures for each charger - see Appendix 2
  1. All sides with some marking—marking must be readable
  2. One side of the charger, if not displayed in the first photo
  3. Top side with the DC plug
  4. Populated PCB (top side)
  5. Populated PCB (bottom side)

Note that if you are providing original/refurbished chargers and are therefore not able to provide the documents mentioned above, we will ask that you share the documentation of your unitary control process.

Please note that we can at all times help you secure high-quality sourcing thanks to our component offer, which includes charging blocks/chargers certified by International Safety Standards (IEC 62368-1). For more information, please reach out to your Back Market point of contact.
Title: Charger compliance with International Safety Standard IEC 62368-1 🔌
